header.stance: Treasuries rallied on lower inflation compensation, but elevated real yields kept policy tight
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

June 9 reversed the prior close’s modest bear steepening without reversing the high-real-rate regime behind it. The two-year Treasury yield fell to 4.13% and the 10-year to 4.53%, as the curve rallied by similar amounts from the front end through the long bond. The composition was more revealing than the size: 10-year breakeven inflation fell two basis points, while real yields eased only slightly. A well-absorbed $58 billion three-year auction then showed that policy-sensitive supply could clear at the newly elevated yield structure.

The rally was therefore a consolidation inside a tightening debate, not a return to expected easing. A sharp decline in crude interrupted the energy impulse that had constrained central banks, and weakness in technology shares supplied a defensive bid for duration. Neither force became a broad flight to safety. The dollar slipped, gold fell, high-yield debt funds gained and the broad equity market lost only modest ground. Markets marked down the marginal inflation premium and protected against risk ahead of consumer-price data without abandoning the view that nominal demand remains firm.

Federal Reserve pricing kept that distinction clear. Options linked to overnight rates targeted one or more increases in coming months, futures embedded a full quarter-point move by year-end, and leveraged short positions had been built to a record before the strong employment release. Cash Treasuries recovered even as those hawkish positions remained dominant. The relief was helped by the possibility of short covering and by client positioning moving toward neutral, but it did not settle whether policy is restrictive enough for an economy supported by hiring and AI-related capital expenditure.

Three transmission channels governed the close. Lower energy prices compressed inflation compensation; softer equities backstopped nominal duration; and strong end-user participation at the three-year sale prevented supply from amplifying the macro uncertainty. Credit markets simultaneously showed abundant capacity for strong borrowers and unusually demanding protections for weaker ones. Abroad, prospective tightening in Europe and Japan, acute Asian funding pressure and oil-importer stress preserved competition for global capital. The central interpretation from the prior close thus continued, but its daily expression changed: the market paused the rise in real yields while leaving the Fed, fiscal-supply and global buyer-base tests unresolved.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasuries rallied in a narrow range across maturities. The two-year yield fell two basis points to 4.13%, the five-year declined three to 4.26%, the 10-year dropped three to 4.53%, and the 30-year eased two to 5.01%. The move modestly flattened 2s10s by one basis point to 40, while 2s30s held at 88. The long bond remained above 5%, and the front end stayed far above the Federal Reserve’s 3.5%-3.75% policy band, so the daily gains relieved pressure without changing the restrictive level of the curve.

Longer comparisons show how little of the prior repricing was unwound. Over one week, yields were still higher by eight basis points at two years, nine at five years, seven at 10 years and four at 30 years. Over one month, the increases were 23, 24, 15 and six basis points. The 2s10s slope was eight basis points flatter over that month and 2s30s was 17 flatter. Policy and neutral-rate uncertainty therefore remained concentrated in the front and belly even after the session’s nearly parallel rally.

Curvature was orderly rather than dislocated. The 2s5s10s butterfly fell one basis point to minus 14, leaving the five-year sector modestly rich relative to its wings after participating most fully in the rally. The 2s10s30s butterfly declined two basis points to minus eight. Those changes fit a market adding duration around the auction sector and trimming inflation protection, rather than one confronting a separate long-end fiscal shock.

Inflation decomposition supplied the cleanest daily signal. The 10-year real yield fell one basis point to 2.20%, while the 10-year breakeven declined two basis points to 2.33%. Five-year real yields were unchanged at 1.82%; seven-, 20- and 30-year real yields each fell one or two basis points. Most of the nominal 10-year rally therefore came from lower inflation compensation. Even so, real yields remained 13 basis points higher over one week at 10 years and 27 higher over one month, preserving a demanding real hurdle for public and private borrowers.

Rate expectations resisted the cash rally. Overnight-rate options accumulated protection against multiple Fed increases, including structures aimed at one or possibly two moves by September. Futures priced a full quarter-point increase by year-end after the employment surprise. Hedge funds had entered that release with a record net short in SOFR futures, although such exposure can also reflect basis and hedging trades. The crowded position created scope for a softer inflation reading to trigger covering, while stronger data could reinforce the same bearish momentum.

Cash positioning was less one-sided. A Treasury client survey showed a small reduction in outright shorts, with the balance shifting to neutral and longs unchanged. That divergence helps explain how coupon yields could fall while hawkish options remained active. Equities weakened enough to support bonds, and lower oil reduced the immediate need to add inflation premium, yet neither development invalidated the post-payroll concern that the neutral policy rate may be higher than officials previously assumed.

The day’s US activity release offered only a mild qualification. April’s goods-and-services trade deficit narrowed to $55.9 billion from a revised $56.6 billion, a little wider than the $55.2 billion public estimate. Exports rose $8.3 billion to $327.1 billion and imports increased $7.6 billion to $383.0 billion. Annual revisions changed the base for second-quarter tracking, but the small net improvement was not large enough to displace employment, inflation and capital spending as the dominant rate inputs.

Bills separated immediate liquidity from the policy horizon. Coupon-equivalent yields ended at 3.66% for four weeks, 3.67% for six weeks and 3.68% for eight weeks, all flat to two basis points lower. The 52-week yield rose eight basis points to 3.90%, extending its one-month increase to 16 basis points. That contrast showed cash remaining orderly at the shortest maturities while the one-year sector carried more of the prospective policy adjustment.

Completed bill auctions confirmed functional funding with some maturity differentiation. Treasury accepted $65.001 billion of 42-day bills at a 3.600% high rate and 3.25 bid-to-cover, close to the prior 3.28 cover on a larger offering. Indirect bidders took 58.67% and dealers 35.80%. The $50 billion 364-day sale cleared at a 3.750% high rate with 3.34 cover, down from 3.41, while indirect participation rose to 68.23% and dealer takedown was 29.59%. Both issues settle June 11; without when-issued references, their cover and allocations define the result.

The three-year auction delivered the stronger coupon-demand signal. The unchanged $58 billion offering cleared at 4.192% with a 4-1/8% coupon and 2.64 bid-to-cover, up from 2.54 in May. Indirect bidders took 63.71%, direct bidders 21.01% and dealers 15.28%. The high yield was well above the prior auction’s 3.965%, but the improved cover and low dealer share showed end users absorbing the policy-sensitive supply at the higher rate structure. Settlement on June 15 leaves a cash event ahead; the auction itself did not reveal pressure in the belly.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit total returns improved despite the technology-led equity decline. LQD gained 0.3239%, helped by its Treasury-duration exposure, while HYG rose 0.1006% and JNK added 0.1253%. Their common positive direction indicates that the session was not a broad withdrawal from corporate risk. The smaller gains in high yield also fit a market distinguishing a government-bond rally from the cash-flow and refinancing questions facing lower-quality borrowers.

Tencent showed how scale, currency choice and a new buyer base could support investment-grade access. The company marketed as much as $4.5 billion of 10- and 20-year dollar bonds and 10- and 30-year offshore yuan notes. Initial guidance was about 80 and 90 basis points over Treasuries for the dollar tranches and roughly 2.95% and 3.55% for the yuan securities. Dollar orders exceeded $3 billion by midmorning, while yuan demand topped 20.5 billion yuan. Final size and pricing remained open at the cutoff, so the orderbooks established interest rather than completed absorption.

The dual-currency structure matched Tencent’s funding needs. The company had $3.5 billion of debt maturing this year and $2.8 billion next year, and planned to use proceeds for refinancing and general purposes including AI development. It had also pledged to more than double annual AI investment to over 36 billion yuan. Expanded access for Chinese insurers to offshore yuan bonds broadened the potential dim-sum buyer base, while the dollar tranches still had to clear against elevated Treasury yields.

Shutterfly occupied the opposite end of the quality spectrum. Investors demanded stronger creditor protections on its $1.15 billion junk bond even at a 12% yield. The revised terms restricted dividends, investments, value transfers and aggressive earnings add-backs. The orderbook was oversubscribed, yet prospective buyers resisted any yield tightening. A $500 million term loan was discussed at 650 basis points over its benchmark with a 96-97 issue price, alongside a $225 million second-lien loan. Access existed, but only with a large premium and tighter control over leakage.

Operating performance explains that resistance. Shutterfly ended March with $2.4 billion of debt; first-quarter revenue fell 8% from a year earlier to $313.5 million, and reported EBITDA losses widened to $19.7 million. AI-generated images and changing media consumption threatened demand even though the company emphasized its physical products. The refinancing followed a prior transaction classified as a distressed exchange, making covenant quality, collateral position and cash generation more decisive than headline oversubscription.

Vedanta’s planned $5.2 billion refinancing illustrated a more constructive route from balance-sheet repair to lower funding costs. The package was intended to replace $3.6 billion of bonds due from 2028 through 2033 and $1.6 billion of loans due from 2028 onward. Net debt had declined to $4.9 billion from $8.9 billion five years earlier, and upgrades from both S&P and Moody’s improved the backdrop. Five-, seven- and 10-year amortizing bonds could smooth maturities, with management targeting as much as a 300-basis-point reduction from borrowing costs near 10%.

European fiber loans exposed the limits of refinancing capacity. Distressed funds bought bank claims on DNS:Net, G.Network and Deutsche Glasfaser as ambitious network rollouts failed to generate enough subscribers and cash flow. One lender sold roughly €350 million of Deutsche Glasfaser loans during a €7 billion restructuring; sponsors contributed €845 million of preferred equity after lenders agreed to subordinate €1.7 billion and provide €400 million of new financing. With about 65% of European fiber companies needing refinancing within two years, nominal infrastructure value no longer substitutes for viable leverage and customer economics.

AI financing remained both an access theme and a source of crowding. CoreWeave held calls with European high-yield investors about possible dollar and euro issuance; its ratings ranged from B+ to BB-, and the euro market had offered little AI-related high-yield supply beyond a recent €800 million data-center bond. Together with Tencent and Shutterfly, the pipeline showed that investors were willing to fund AI beneficiaries, but sharply differentiated borrowers exposed to AI disruption. The relevant clearing variables were currency, covenant package, security rank and cash-flow visibility, not the technology label alone.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Germany’s April production data softened the European growth impulse without delivering an outright contraction. Output rose 0.4% from March after a revised 0.1% decline, just below the 0.5% public estimate. Construction increased 2.4%, but manufacturing excluding energy and construction was flat and auto production fell 4.7%. Output was down 0.5% over both the year and the latest three-month comparison. Bund duration therefore received support from a weak industrial core even as the energy shock constrained the policy response.

France’s central bank sharpened that tension. Governor Emmanuel Moulin said its next projections would show less growth and higher inflation because the Iran conflict had made the oil shock larger and more persistent than previously assumed. The prior French inflation baseline was 1.7% for 2026, while the economy had contracted in the first quarter and business indicators were deteriorating. Markets still fully priced another quarter-point ECB increase during the year and leaned toward an additional move, keeping euro front-end yields competitive with Treasuries despite softer activity.

Japanese policy pressure came from both inflation and credibility. A former Bank of Japan executive argued that the central bank was falling behind the curve as businesses passed higher costs to customers more quickly. Japan’s 10-year yield had reached 2.8% in May, its highest since 1996, amid concern about fiscal expansion and delayed tightening. Expectations of further BOJ action raise the local return available to Japanese institutions and can lift the hedged yield needed for foreign duration to remain attractive.

Fiscal management offered a partial offset. Japan’s ¥3.1 trillion supplemental budget addressed inflation and the war’s effects without adding to calendar-year bond issuance. The government also proposed reducing routine reliance on extra budgets and focusing on the debt-to-GDP ratio, which exceeds 200%. Moving programs into an initial budget currently near ¥122 trillion could improve transparency, but a visibly larger headline budget may still unsettle JGB investors. Communication and the ultimate issuance path matter more for duration than the accounting label.

Taiwan demonstrated how policy expectations and cash scarcity can reinforce one another. The five-year government yield jumped 32 basis points to 1.71%, its highest since 2008, and a 10-year auction produced the highest yield since 2013. Seasonal tax payments drained banking-system liquidity, while outstanding negotiable certificates of deposit fell to the lowest level since 2014 as lenders conserved cash. Above-threshold inflation then increased expectations that policy would remain restrictive, turning a funding squeeze into a broader curve repricing.

Europe’s effort to deepen the euro’s international role added a slower-moving supply channel. Moulin argued that a larger pool of euro safe assets, potentially including common borrowing for defense and AI projects, would be necessary to attract more reserve, transaction and issuance demand. A deeper benchmark market could broaden the global duration buyer base over time. It would also add competing high-grade supply. For Treasuries, the near-term ECB path affects relative yields; the longer-term question is whether euro issuance becomes a more substantial alternative destination for global reserves.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

The external backdrop offered limited daily relief to emerging-market debt. EMB rose 0.4093%, the dollar index slipped 0.1399%, and both crude benchmarks fell sharply. That combination eased the immediate base-rate, currency and terms-of-trade pressure facing oil importers. It did not erase country-specific stress: the fund’s one-week return remained negative, and local policy credibility continued to dominate several sovereign curves.

Indonesia’s off-cycle tightening failed its most demanding market test. Bank Indonesia raised its policy rate 25 basis points to 5.5%, taking cumulative increases over three weeks to 75 basis points. The rupiah recovered 0.7% and equities gained more than 7%, but the 10-year government yield climbed 23 basis points to 7.51%. Higher carry supported the currency and stocks; bonds continued to price fiscal intervention, an expanded central-bank growth mandate and uncertainty over economic governance.

The central bank explicitly sought higher yields to attract foreign inflows, even as intervention depleted reserves for a fifth consecutive month. The rupiah remained nearly 8% weaker for the year after touching successive record lows. Coordination with the government may stabilize markets temporarily, but it blurs the boundary between currency defense, bond-market support and growth policy. The local sovereign selloff therefore reflected confidence and institutional risk in addition to the mechanical effect of a higher policy rate.

Kenya chose to preserve its existing restraint. The central bank held its benchmark at 8.75% after 10 earlier reductions totaling 425 basis points. Headline inflation had accelerated to 6.7% from 5.6% and core inflation to 3.2% from 2.8%, as diesel and gasoline costs rose 40% and 20% since the war began. A stable shilling and $13.2 billion of reserves, covering almost six months of imports, supplied a buffer, but projected growth was cut to 4.9% and the current-account deficit was expected to widen to 3% of GDP.

Senegal remained a debt-management case rather than a broad EM beta story. The sovereign’s 2031 dollar yield reached 24.56%, matching a record, and its hard-currency premium exceeded 1,500 basis points over Treasuries. S&P maintained a negative outlook on the CCC+ foreign-currency rating, while financing needs were estimated near 30% of GDP. The absence of a restored IMF program after undisclosed debt led to suspension of a $1.8 billion facility left the country effectively shut out of international capital markets.

Reliance on regional markets creates a second-order risk. International investors hold exposure through banks in the West African monetary union, including Ivorian institutions, so Senegal’s funding stress can migrate through balance sheets even without a new eurobond. Domestic investors hold about 73% of local debt and deposit-funded bank liquidity remains an anchor. That buyer base reduces immediate rollover instability, but it also concentrates sovereign risk inside the regional financial system.

The EM picture was consequently split three ways. Indonesia paid a higher policy rate without winning a bond rally; Kenya retained currency stability but faced deteriorating oil arithmetic; Senegal lacked market access and depended on a credible multilateral path. A softer dollar and lower crude improved the common backdrop for one session. Usable reserves, institutional clarity and maturity financing still determined whether that relief reached each sovereign curve.

## Cross asset

Oil provided the strongest confirmation of the Treasury rally. WTI fell 3.3954% to $88.20 and Brent declined 2.9708% to $91.45 as a halt in Iran-Israel strikes improved the immediate supply-risk outlook. The drop aligned with the two-basis-point decline in 10-year breakevens and reduced the marginal pressure on policy-sensitive yields. Crude remained high enough for European and EM central banks to worry about second-round effects, so the daily move lowered the inflation impulse without resolving it.

Equities supplied a modest defensive channel rather than a liquidation signal. The S&P 500 lost 0.2576%, the Nasdaq 100 fell 1.1211% and the semiconductor index declined 1.9283%. The VIX rose 5.0211% to 19.87. Technology’s underperformance matched concern about elevated real rates, issuance needs and AI valuations, while the much smaller broad-index decline indicated that investors were rotating away from long-duration earnings rather than abandoning nominal growth altogether.

The dollar and gold resisted a classic haven interpretation. The dollar index eased 0.1399% to 99.91, and gold futures fell 1.7505% to $4,260. Earlier, copper rose as Middle East tensions eased and Chinese exports grew more than 19% from a year earlier, supported by AI-hardware demand. Those moves point to lower immediate geopolitical protection and continued industrial demand, not a synchronized expectation of recession.

Fixed-income risk gauges completed the mixed configuration. Investment-grade, high-yield and emerging-market debt funds all rose, with the largest gain in EMB and a meaningful duration benefit in LQD. Their performance reflected different combinations of government yields, carry and risk appetite. Together with a softer dollar and only modest broad-equity weakness, the gains show that the Treasury rally coexisted with functioning credit demand. Lower inflation compensation, not systemic deleveraging, was the cleaner cross-asset explanation.

## Forward Market Significance

May CPI on June 10 at 08:30 ET, followed by PPI and jobless claims on June 11 at 08:30, will test whether June 9’s breakeven-led rally can survive realized inflation. CPI is expected to show headline gains of 0.5% on the month and 4.2% on the year after 0.6% and 3.8%, with core expected at 0.2% monthly and 2.9% yearly after 0.4% and 2.8%. PPI is expected to slow to 0.7% for final demand and 0.5% excluding food and energy from revised 1.4% and 1.0% monthly increases. The observable trigger is whether energy remains concentrated in headline prices or broadens through core goods, services and producer margins, with claims showing whether the prior 225,000 reading was noise or persistent weakening. Softer core breadth and firmer claims would confirm the lower-breakeven, front-end relief interpretation and encourage short covering. Broad inflation pressure with stable labor demand would challenge it, validate hawkish options and place two- to 10-year real yields back under pressure.

The remaining coupon sequence will test whether the three-year auction’s strong end-user absorption extends into duration after the inflation releases. Treasury reopens $39 billion of 10-year notes at 13:00 ET on June 10 and $22 billion of 30-year bonds at 13:00 on June 11; the three coupon issues settle together on June 15 for $119 billion gross. Clearing levels against then-current when-issued yields, bid-to-cover, direct and indirect shares, dealer takedown and any settlement strain are the observable triggers. Strong bidding across the 10- and 30-year sales would confirm that June 9 was chiefly an inflation-premium adjustment and resolve part of the buyer-base concern left by elevated real yields. A weak long-end result, especially after benign inflation, would challenge that account and reopen fiscal supply, dealer capacity and competing corporate issuance as independent causes of term-premium pressure.

The ECB decision on June 11 at 08:15 ET and the Bank of Japan decision on June 16 will test whether global central banks translate the oil shock into synchronized tightening or distinguish first-round inflation from weaker activity. The public baseline expects the ECB deposit rate to rise 25 basis points to 2.25% and the BOJ target to rise 25 basis points to about 1.00%. Observable triggers are the projected inflation path, guidance on follow-up moves, JGB and Bund curve reactions, and whether the yen and euro strengthen without disorderly long-end selling. A contained ECB sequence and a credible BOJ response would challenge the view that foreign curves must keep lifting the Treasury clearing rate. Hawkish follow-through, renewed oil gains or poor long-duration demand abroad would confirm global competition for capital and reopen pressure on oil-importing EM currencies and US term yields.

section_summaries.market_read: The close was a pause in real-yield pressure, not confirmation that restrictive policy had peaked.
section_summaries.next_check: The next test is whether inflation data and longer auctions validate the breakeven-led rally.
section_summaries.summary: June 9’s Treasury rally reflected lower inflation compensation, softer technology shares and clean three-year supply absorption, not a reversal of the high-real-rate regime. Credit remained functional and the dollar eased, while hawkish Fed pricing, fiscal supply and competition for global capital left the broader tightening debate unresolved.

section_summaries.market_read: Rates relief came from lower inflation premium, while the restrictive real-rate hurdle persisted.
section_summaries.next_check: Core inflation breadth and demand at the remaining coupon sales will show whether relief can extend.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: A near-parallel rally modestly flattened the curve, with breakevens doing more work than real yields. Weekly and monthly yield increases remained largely intact, hawkish rate options survived the cash move, and strong three-year demand showed the belly could absorb supply at its elevated rate structure.

section_summaries.market_read: Corporate demand stayed open, but borrower quality and covenant protection determined clearing terms.
section_summaries.next_check: Final Tencent pricing and weaker-borrower terms will test whether selective demand is tightening.
section_summaries.summary: Positive credit-fund returns and robust investment-grade orderbooks showed that financing remained available, but access was sharply quality-sensitive. Tencent broadened its buyer base, while Shutterfly faced costly terms and tighter protections; stressed fiber refinancing underscored that leverage and cash-flow visibility still governed absorption.

section_summaries.market_read: Foreign curves remained a source of competition for Treasury buyers despite softer growth signals.
section_summaries.next_check: ECB and BOJ guidance, together with Bund and JGB long-end reactions, will test that competition.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Soft German activity supported duration, but Europe’s oil-driven inflation outlook and expected ECB tightening kept front-end yields competitive. Japan’s prospective tightening and fiscal credibility raised the hurdle for foreign bonds, while Taiwan’s liquidity squeeze showed how cash scarcity can amplify a restrictive policy signal.

section_summaries.market_read: EM duration remained country-specific, with policy credibility outweighing a friendlier global backdrop.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether Indonesia’s higher carry stabilizes bonds and whether Senegal restores a multilateral path.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: A softer dollar and cheaper crude improved the common backdrop, but relief remained subordinate to domestic credibility and funding access. Indonesia tightened without securing a bond rally, Kenya preserved currency stability amid worsening oil costs, and Senegal’s market exclusion concentrated risk in regional banks.

section_summaries.market_read: Cross-asset signals supported a disinflationary duration bid without showing broad deleveraging.
section_summaries.next_check: Renewed oil gains or broader equity stress would distinguish inflation reversal from risk liquidation.
section_summaries.summary: Falling crude aligned with lower breakevens, while technology-led equity weakness created a modest defensive bid for Treasuries. A softer dollar, lower gold and gains across credit funds argued against systemic flight, leaving reduced inflation pressure rather than recession fear as the cleaner explanation.

section_summaries.market_read: The rally remains conditional on inflation breadth, duration demand and restrained global tightening.
section_summaries.next_check: The first observable check is May CPI and the reopened 10-year auction on June 10.
section_summaries.summary: Three linked tests will determine whether the rally extends: US inflation and labor data, long-duration Treasury auctions, and ECB and BOJ decisions. Benign inflation with strong auction demand would validate the move, while broad price pressure, weak long-end bidding or hawkish global follow-through would restore real-yield and term-premium pressure.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.2% (-1 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.33% (-2 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 40 bp (-1 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 88 bp (0 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Treasuries rallied as lower oil compressed inflation compensation and supply cleared cleanly
overall_summary.session_narrative: At the completed NY close, Treasury yields fell across maturities as crude dropped and technology shares weakened, trimming inflation compensation and adding a defensive duration bid. The three-year auction cleared with stronger end-user participation, showing that policy-sensitive supply could be absorbed at elevated yields. Yet real rates stayed high, Fed hike protection remained active, and overseas policy and funding pressures limited the rally’s significance.
overall_summary.summary: The curve rallied nearly in parallel, led by a decline in breakevens rather than a decisive easing in real rates. Strong three-year demand and modest equity weakness supported duration, while hawkish Fed pricing, fiscal supply and global buyer competition kept the broader tightening debate intact.

watch_events.market_implication: The first mix supports short covering; the second revives pressure on two- to 10-year real yields.
watch_events.market_trigger: Softer core breadth with firmer claims, or broad inflation with stable labor demand.
watch_events.title: US inflation and labor-data test
watch_events.watch_line: Track whether energy pressure broadens into core prices as claims confirm or refute labor softness.

watch_events.market_implication: Strong demand validates the inflation-premium rally; weakness reopens fiscal and dealer-capacity pressure.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad end-user demand across both sales, or a weak long-end result despite benign inflation.
watch_events.title: Long-duration Treasury auction test
watch_events.watch_line: Assess 10- and 30-year clearing, bidder shares and dealer takedown after the inflation releases.

watch_events.market_implication: Orderly outcomes ease competition for Treasury buyers; hawkishness lifts global term-yield pressure.
watch_events.market_trigger: Contained guidance with orderly curves, or hawkish follow-through and renewed oil gains.
watch_events.title: ECB and BOJ tightening test
watch_events.watch_line: Watch policy guidance, projected inflation and Bund, JGB, euro and yen reactions.
